name: Makonde
description: The Makonde inhabit the Mtwara Region of south-east Tanzania (and northern Mozambique), isolated on a high plateau that historically protected them from slavers. They are most famous globally for their ebony wood sculpture — intricate, multi-figure carvings depicting the Ujamaa (family tree) concept and the supernatural. Their woodcarving tradition, recognised by UNESCO, is a living artistic heritage that has influenced contemporary African art worldwide. Matrilineal and matrilocal, they represent one of Africa's most intact matrilineal societies.
cultural_highlights: Ujamaa sculpture — interconnected multi-figure ebony carvings depicting family, community, and the unity of generations; a UNESCO-recognised art form
Shetani carvings — abstract spirit figures with elongated, distorted forms representing the Makonde spirit world; groundbreaking in African contemporary art
Mapiko masquerade — a male initiation masquerade featuring elaborate wooden masks; strictly secret and performed only during male circumcision ceremonies
Matrilineal society — children inherit clan identity through the mother; residency after marriage is with the wife's family (matrilocal)

food_traditions.name: Ugali with cassava leaves
food_traditions.description: Cassava leaf stew (mchuzi wa kisamvu) with ugali — the standard Makonde plateau meal

food_traditions.name: Dried fish and coconut rice
food_traditions.description: Coastal influence in Makonde cooking; coconut-based rice dishes reflect Indian Ocean trade connections

food_traditions.name: Maharagwe (kidney beans)
food_traditions.description: Slow-cooked red kidney beans with coconut milk — a filling daily meal in the dry plateau

music_traditions: Lipala dance — a social dance performed at celebrations with call-and-response singing and polyrhythmic drumming
Sindimba — a traditional Makonde dance performed during festivals, known for the deep back-bending of dancers
Mapiko drum ensemble — ceremonial drums that announce the masquerade spirits during initiation ceremonies
